section.title: Joseph and Potiphar's House
section.sectionRationale: Sold into Egypt, Joseph rises in Potiphar's house because the LORD is with him. When his master's wife tempts him, he refuses out of loyalty and reverence for God, and is falsely accused and imprisoned, yet the LORD is with him still. The chapter is framed by the refrain of God's abiding presence.
section.thesis: The LORD's presence, not outward circumstance, is the true blessing; He prospers His faithful servant in a slave's house and a prison cell alike, sustaining integrity under temptation and injustice on the road to a saving purpose.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the LORD with Joseph in Potiphar's house; the temptation resisted; the false accusation and imprisonment; and the LORD with Joseph in prison.

verses.text: Meanwhile, Joseph had been taken down to Egypt, where an Egyptian named Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh and captain of the guard, bought him from the Ishmaelites who had taken him there.
And the LORD was with Joseph, and he became a {{tsalach|successful}} man, serving in the household of his Egyptian master.
When his master saw that the LORD was with him and made him prosper in all he did,
Joseph found {{chen|favor}} in his sight and became his personal attendant. Potiphar put him in charge of his household and entrusted him with everything he owned.

From the time that he put Joseph in charge of his household and all he owned, the LORD blessed the Egyptian’s household on account of him. The LORD’s blessing was on everything he owned, both in his house and in his field.
So Potiphar left all that he owned in Joseph’s care; he did not concern himself with anything except the food he ate. Now Joseph was well-built and handsome,

units.label: Temptation resisted
units.note: how could I sin against God

verses.text: and after some time his master’s wife cast her eyes upon Joseph and said, “Sleep with me.”
But he refused. “Look,” he said to his master’s wife, “with me here, my master does not concern himself with anything in his house, and he has entrusted everything he owns to my care.
No one in this house is greater than I am. He has withheld nothing from me except you, because you are his wife. So how could I do such a great evil and {{chata|sin against God}}?”
Although Potiphar’s wife spoke to Joseph day after day, he refused to go to bed with her or even be near her.
One day, however, Joseph went into the house to attend to his work, and not a single household servant was inside.
She grabbed Joseph by his {{beged|cloak}} and said, “Sleep with me!” But leaving his cloak in her hand, he escaped and ran outside.

units.label: False accusation and prison
units.note: the cloak used as a lie

verses.text: When she saw that he had left his cloak in her hand and had run out of the house,
she called her household servants. “Look,” she said, “this Hebrew has been brought to us to make sport of us. He came to me so he could sleep with me, but I screamed as loud as I could.
When he heard me scream for help, he left his cloak beside me and ran out of the house.”
So Potiphar’s wife kept Joseph’s cloak beside her until his master came home.
Then she told him the same story: “The Hebrew slave you brought us came to me to make sport of me,
but when I screamed for help, he left his cloak beside me and ran out of the house.”
When his master heard the story his wife told him, saying, “This is what your slave did to me,” he burned with anger.
So Joseph’s master took him and had him thrown into the prison where the king’s prisoners were confined. While Joseph was there in the prison,

units.label: The LORD with Joseph in prison

verses.text: the LORD was with him and extended kindness to him, granting him favor in the eyes of the prison warden.
And the warden put all the prisoners under Joseph’s care, so that he was responsible for all that was done in the prison.
The warden did not concern himself with anything under Joseph’s care, because the LORD was with Joseph and gave him success in whatever he did.

tsalach.gloss: To succeed, prosper, or accomplish. In this chapter it describes Joseph in slavery and in prison, not in comfort. His prospering is not freedom from hardship but the fruitfulness God grants a faithful servant even in bondage, because the LORD is with him.

chen.gloss: Favor or grace, the goodwill one finds in another's eyes. Joseph gains favor with Potiphar and later with the prison warden, but the source is named plainly: the LORD grants it. The word points beyond human liking to the gracious hand of God who lifts the lowly.

chata.gloss: To sin, to miss the mark. Joseph names adultery not merely as a betrayal of Potiphar but as a great evil and sin against God Himself. His conscience is governed by the LORD's presence, showing that true purity flows from reverence for God, not just fear of getting caught.

beged.gloss: A garment or cloak. For the second time a garment of Joseph's is stripped away and used to lie about him, first the robe dipped in blood, now the cloak left in the woman's hand. The repeated motif marks Joseph as the falsely accused sufferer whose innocence God will vindicate.

basic.context: Joseph is now a slave in the house of Potiphar, an Egyptian official. Because the LORD is with him, he prospers and is put in charge of the whole household. When Potiphar's wife repeatedly tries to seduce him, he refuses. She falsely accuses him, and he is thrown into prison, where again the LORD is with him and gives him favor.
basic.hermeneutics: The key question is what it means that the LORD was with Joseph, since his life goes from slavery to false imprisonment. The chapter answers that God's presence is the real blessing, sustaining Joseph's integrity and fruitfulness even when his circumstances are unjust and hard.
basic.originalLanguages: The word for successful describes Joseph in slavery and prison, not ease. He finds favor, but the text says God grants it. Joseph calls adultery a sin against God, not just against his master. And once more a garment of his, his cloak, is seized and used to lie about him, marking him as the falsely accused.
basic.typology: Joseph is a clear type of Christ: the righteous servant who suffers unjustly, resists evil, is falsely accused, and is numbered with prisoners, yet is with God and finally vindicated. His purity and his descent into the prison prefigure the sinless Sufferer who goes down before He is raised.
basic.christ: Like Joseph, Jesus was the righteous one who did no wrong, was falsely accused, and was handed over to suffering, yet God was with Him throughout. Joseph's refusal to sin points to Christ's perfect obedience, and his unjust imprisonment points to the greater descent of the innocent Savior.
basic.god: The refrain the LORD was with Joseph frames the whole chapter. God is present in the slave quarters and in the dungeon, granting favor, blessing, and success. His presence does not remove hardship but transforms it, proving that nearness to God, not comfort, is the deepest good.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 105 says God tested Joseph until his word came true. Romans 8 promises that God works all things for good and that nothing can separate us from His love. Hebrews 13 assures believers that God will never leave nor forsake them.
basic.oneStory: Joseph's faithful suffering is one more step on the road by which God will raise him to save many. His integrity under temptation and his innocence under accusation display the character of the coming Redeemer, and the abiding presence of the God who is with His people in every pit.

context.literary: The chapter is bracketed by the refrain the LORD was with Joseph, at the start in Potiphar's house and at the end in prison, forming a deliberate frame that interprets everything between. It resumes the Joseph narrative interrupted by Genesis 38 and contrasts Joseph's purity with Judah's earlier failure.
context.historical: Potiphar was an Egyptian official, captain of the guard. Household slaves could rise to positions of great trust and administration in Egyptian estates. The prison for the king's prisoners was likely attached to the captain's own compound, which is why Joseph later interacts with royal officials there.
context.canonical: The motif of the righteous sufferer whom God is with runs from Joseph through the Psalms and prophets to Christ. The chapter's emphasis on God's presence in affliction undergirds later biblical assurances of God with us, fulfilled in Immanuel and in the indwelling Spirit.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: Moses shows that God's covenant presence goes with Joseph into slavery and prison, sustaining his integrity and prospering him for God's saving purpose, teaching Israel that the LORD is faithfully with His people even in unjust suffering.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes Joseph's conduct in a way clearly meant to be admired: his flight from temptation and his refusal to sin against God are exemplary. Yet the narrative does not promise that righteousness will always be rewarded with ease; Joseph's obedience lands him in prison.

debates.question: Does the LORD was with Joseph mean God's people should expect outward success and ease?

views.label: Presence, not prosperity as comfort
views.case: Joseph prospers precisely while enslaved and imprisoned; the blessing is God's nearness and fruitfulness in hardship, not release from it. The refrain frames a chapter that ends in a dungeon, ruling out a health-and-wealth reading.

views.label: God grants genuine favor and advancement
views.case: The text does show real, tangible favor: Joseph is elevated in the house and in the prison, and God visibly blesses what he touches, so God's presence often bears outward fruit, though never guaranteeing ease.

debates.bearing: Both agree the deepest good is God with us. Believers may trust God's real blessing and providence without expecting immunity from injustice, following Joseph's faithfulness whatever the outcome.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter is governed by the refrain the LORD was with Joseph. He becomes tsalach, successful, but the word describes him as a slave and a prisoner, so prospering here means fruitfulness under God's hand, not comfort. He is given chen, favor, yet the narrator insists the LORD grants it, pointing past human goodwill to grace. Joseph names the tempting sin as chata against God, showing that his purity springs from reverence for the God who is present. And his beged, his cloak, is seized and twisted into a lie, echoing the bloodied robe and marking Joseph as the innocent, falsely accused sufferer.

typology.type: The righteous servant who suffers unjustly yet has God with him
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the sinless one who suffered wrongfully while the Father was with Him, and was vindicated in resurrection.

typology.type: Joseph falsely accused and numbered with prisoners
typology.fulfillment: Jesus falsely charged and numbered with transgressors, innocent yet condemned.

typology.type: Joseph's descent into the prison before his exaltation
typology.fulfillment: The pattern of Christ's humiliation before His glorification, going down before being raised up.

study.christ: Joseph shines here as a portrait of Christ. He is the servant in whom God delights, faithful in another's house, tempted yet without sin, who counts adultery a great evil against God. Falsely accused and stripped of his garment, he is thrown among prisoners though innocent, just as Jesus was charged with crimes He never committed and numbered with transgressors. And as the LORD was with Joseph in the dungeon, so the Father was with the Son in His suffering, bringing Him through humiliation to the exaltation from which He saves. Joseph's purity foreshadows the perfect obedience of the One who resisted every temptation for us.
study.god: The chapter's heartbeat is the LORD was with Joseph, sounded over both his rise and his fall. God's presence does not spare Joseph from slavery or prison, but it fills those places with favor, blessing, and fruitfulness. He is the God who goes down with His servant into the pit and the cell, who governs Egyptian masters and prison wardens alike, and who is quietly steering even injustice toward a purpose of salvation. His nearness, not Joseph's comfort, is the true and enduring blessing.

crossReferences.note: Joseph later sees God's saving hand behind all his suffering, sent to preserve life.
God sent Joseph and tested him until the time that his word came to pass.
God works all things together for the good of those who love Him, as in Joseph's ordeal.
Flee sexual immorality; the body belongs to God, echoing Joseph's flight and reverence.

study.oneStory: Joseph's slavery and imprisonment are dark, unjust steps, yet each is a stride toward the day he will save many from famine. The refrain the LORD was with him teaches that God's presence, not ease, is the great gift, and Joseph's faithful suffering sketches the coming Redeemer who was innocent, accused, and forsaken by men, but never by God, and who through humiliation brought the world its salvation.

quiz.q: What does the refrain the LORD was with Joseph mean, given where the chapter ends?

options.text: God's presence brought favor and fruitfulness even in slavery and prison, not freedom from hardship.
options.note: The refrain frames a chapter that ends in a dungeon, ruling out ease as the blessing.

options.text: God guaranteed Joseph a comfortable and trouble-free life.
options.note: Joseph's life is full of trouble; the blessing is God's presence, not comfort.

options.text: God was with Joseph only while he was prospering, then left him.
options.note: The refrain returns in prison, showing God stayed with him throughout.

options.text: God's presence made Joseph immune to temptation and accusation.
options.note: He was tempted and falsely accused; God's presence sustained him through both.

quiz.q: On what grounds did Joseph refuse Potiphar's wife?

options.text: It would be a great evil and a sin against God, as well as a betrayal of his master.
options.note: His purity flowed from reverence for God, verse 9.

options.text: He was simply afraid of being caught and punished.
options.note: Fear of consequences is not his stated reason; he names sin against God.

options.text: He found Potiphar's wife unattractive.
options.note: The issue is moral and God-centered, not personal preference.

options.text: He was indifferent and did not care either way.
options.note: He refused firmly and repeatedly, day after day.

quiz.q: How does the motif of Joseph's garment develop the story?

options.text: For a second time a garment of his is seized and used to lie about him.
options.note: First the bloodied robe, now the cloak, marking him as the falsely accused.

options.text: His cloak proves his innocence to Potiphar at once.
options.note: The cloak is used against him, not to clear him; he is imprisoned.

options.text: The garment is a reward from Pharaoh for his service.
options.note: That royal clothing comes later; here the cloak becomes false evidence.

options.text: The garment plays no meaningful role in the chapter.
options.note: It is central, the very object twisted into a lie against him.

quiz.q: How is Joseph in this chapter a picture of Christ?

options.text: The righteous servant who resists sin, is falsely accused, and suffers with God still present.
options.note: The pattern mirrors Christ's innocence, false charges, and the Father's presence.

options.text: A ruler who overthrows Egypt by military conquest.
options.note: That is not this chapter; the likeness is innocent suffering, not conquest.

options.text: A tempter who leads others into sin.
options.note: Joseph resists temptation; he does not tempt others.

options.text: A man who compromises to escape prison.
options.note: Joseph refuses to compromise, and it costs him prison.

quiz.q: What deeper good does the chapter hold out to believers who suffer unjustly?

options.text: The abiding presence of God, who is with His people in every pit and cell.
options.note: God's nearness, not circumstance, is the true blessing, echoed in Hebrews 13:5.

options.text: The promise that the faithful will never face injustice.
options.note: Joseph faces deep injustice; the promise is God's presence, not immunity.

options.text: The assurance of immediate rescue from every trial.
options.note: Joseph's vindication takes years; God is present long before release comes.

options.text: The guarantee of wealth for the godly.
options.note: Joseph is a slave and prisoner; the good is God with him, not riches.

questions: Joseph called adultery a sin against God, not just against people. How would seeing your temptations first as matters between you and God reshape the way you resist them?
The LORD was with Joseph in the slave house and the prison. Where do you most need to believe that God is present with you right now, even in an unjust or confining place?
Joseph fled temptation rather than reasoning with it. What would practical fleeing look like for you, and where do you tend to linger instead?
Joseph did the right thing and it cost him his freedom. How do you keep trusting God when obedience seems to make your situation worse, not better?
Joseph's innocent suffering points to Christ, who was falsely accused for you. How does gazing at the greater Sufferer strengthen you to stay faithful in your own trials?

translationNotes.note: Successful renders a Hebrew word for prospering or accomplishing; in context it describes fruitfulness under God's hand while Joseph is a slave, not material ease.
Make sport of us carries a mocking, contemptuous sense; Potiphar's wife plays on ethnic prejudice by stressing that Joseph is a Hebrew brought among them.
The prison where the king's prisoners were confined was likely attached to the captain of the guard's own house, which sets up Joseph's contact with Pharaoh's officials in the next chapter.
